question.text: Under FEMA 1999, what is the threshold that distinguishes Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI) from Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in an Indian company?

answer.text: FPI is investment of 10% or more, while FDI is less than 10% of post-issue paid-up equity
FPI is investment of less than 10%, while FDI is 10% or more of post-issue paid-up equity
FPI is investment of less than 25%, while FDI is 25% or more of post-issue paid-up equity
There is no specific threshold; the distinction is based on the investor's intent

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under FEMA 1999, Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI) refers to investment of less than 10% of the post-issue paid-up equity capital of an Indian company, while Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) is investment of 10% or more of the post-issue paid-up equity capital. **The Trap:** The first option reverses the definitions of FPI and FDI, which is a common confusion. Also, 25% is a threshold used in other contexts (like substantial acquisition under SEBI Takeover Code), not for FPI/FDI distinction. **Cross-Exam Context:** This threshold is critical for NISM Series V-A, X-A, and X-B exams, and is also relevant for NISM Depository Operations certification. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Remember the simple rule — below 10% is portfolio (passive), at or above 10% is direct (active control). This distinction affects regulatory approvals, reporting requirements, and exit routes.
